fields.name: buf_attach_client
documentation: Implements the `textDocument/did…` notifications required to track a buffer
Without calling this, the server won't be notified of changes to a buffer.
@*return* `success` — `true` if client was attached successfully; `false` otherwise

fields.name: buf_detach_client
documentation: Detaches client from the specified buffer.
Note: While the server is notified that the text document (buffer)
was closed, it is still able to send notifications should it ignore this notification.

fields.name: buf_get_clients
documentation: Gets a map of client_id:client pairs for the given buffer, where each value

fields.name: buf_is_attached
documentation: Checks if a buffer is attached for a particular client.

fields.name: buf_notify
documentation: function lsp.buf_notify(bufnr: integer|nil, method: string, params: any)
Send a notification to a server
@*param* `params` — Arguments to send to the server
@*return* `success` — true if any client returns true; false otherwise

fields.name: buf_request
documentation: function lsp.buf_request(bufnr: integer, method: string, params?: table|fun(client: vim.lsp.Client, bufnr: integer):table?, handler?: fun(err?: lsp.ResponseError, result: any, context: lsp.HandlerContext, config?: table):...unknown, on_unsupported?: fun())
Sends an async request for all active clients attached to the
@*param* `params` — Parameters to send to the server
If nil, follows resolution strategy defined in |lsp-handler-configuration|
@*return* `_cancel_all_requests` — Function which can be used to
cancel all the requests. You could instead
iterate all clients and call their `cancel_request()` methods.

fields.name: buf_request_all
documentation: function lsp.buf_request_all(bufnr: integer, method: string, params?: table|fun(client: vim.lsp.Client, bufnr: integer):table?, handler: fun(results: table<integer, { err: (lsp.ResponseError)?, result: any }>, context: lsp.HandlerContext, config?: table):...unknown)
Sends an async request for all active clients attached to the buffer and executes the `handler`
@*param* `params` — Parameters to send to the server.
Can also be passed as a function that returns the params table for cases where
parameters are specific to the client.
Handler called after all requests are completed. Server results are passed as

fields.name: buf_request_sync
documentation: function lsp.buf_request_sync(bufnr: integer, method: string, params?: table, timeout_ms?: integer)
Sends a request to all server and waits for the response of all of them.
Calls |vim.lsp.buf_request_all()| but blocks Nvim while awaiting the result.
Parameters are the same as |vim.lsp.buf_request_all()| but the result is
@*param* `params` — Parameters to send to the server
@*param* `timeout_ms` — Maximum time in milliseconds to wait for a result.
@*return* `err` — On timeout, cancel, or error, `err` is a string describing the failure reason, and `result` is nil.

fields.name: client_errors
documentation: Error codes to be used with `on_error` from |vim.lsp.start_client|.
Can be used to look up the string from a the number or the number

fields.name: client_is_stopped
documentation: Checks whether a client is stopped.
@*return* `stopped` — true if client is stopped, false otherwise.

fields.name: commands
documentation: This is an extension point for plugins to handle custom commands which are
not part of the core language server protocol specification.
The registry is a table where the key is a unique command name,
and the value is a function which is called if any LSP action
(code action, code lenses, ...) triggers the command.
If an LSP response contains a command for which no matching entry is
available in this registry, the command will be executed via the LSP server
The first argument to the function will be the `Command`:
The second argument is the `ctx` of |lsp-handler|

fields.name: enable
documentation: Auto-starts LSP when a buffer is opened, based on the |lsp-config| `filetypes`, `root_markers`,
Example: [lsp-restart](file:///usr/local/share/nvim/runtime/lua/vim) Passing `false` stops and detaches the client(s). Thus you can
"restart" LSP by disabling and re-enabling a given config:
Example: To _dynamically_ decide whether LSP is activated, define a |lsp-root_dir()| function
which calls `on_dir()` only when you want that config to activate:
@*param* `enable` — `true|nil` to enable, `false` to disable (actively stops and detaches

fields.name: foldclose
documentation: Close all {kind} of folds in the the window with {winid}.
To automatically fold imports when opening a file, you can use an autocmd:
@*param* `kind` — Kind to close, one of "comment", "imports" or "region".
-- A set of predefined range kinds.

fields.name: foldexpr
documentation: Provides an interface between the built-in client and a `foldexpr` function.
To use, set 'foldmethod' to "expr" and set the value of 'foldexpr':
Or use it only when supported by checking for the "textDocument/foldingRange"
-- Prefer LSP folding if client supports it

fields.name: foldtext
documentation: Provides a `foldtext` function that shows the `collapsedText` retrieved,
defaults to the first folded line if `collapsedText` is not provided.

fields.name: for_each_buffer_client
documentation: Invokes a function for each LSP client attached to a buffer.
@*param* `fn` — Function to run on each client attached to buffer
{bufnr}. The function takes the client, client ID, and

fields.name: formatexpr
documentation: Provides an interface between the built-in client and a `formatexpr` function.
Currently only supports a single client. This can be set via

fields.name: get_buffers_by_client_id
documentation: Returns list of buffers attached to client_id.

fields.name: get_client_by_id
documentation: Gets a client by id, or nil if the id is invalid.
The returned client may not yet be fully initialized.

fields.name: get_log_path
documentation: Gets the path of the logfile used by the LSP client.

fields.name: handlers
documentation: (global) vim.lsp.handlers: table<string, fun(err?: lsp.ResponseError, result: any, context: lsp.HandlerContext, config?: table):...unknown>

fields.name: is_enabled
documentation: Checks if the given LSP config is enabled (globally, not per-buffer).
Unlike `vim.lsp.config['…']`, this does not have the side-effect of resolving the config.

fields.name: log_levels
documentation: Log level dictionary with reverse lookup as well.
Can be used to lookup the number from the name or the

fields.name: omnifunc
documentation: - findstart=0: column where the completion starts, or -2 or -3
- findstart=1: list of matches (actually just calls |complete()|)

fields.name: protocol
documentation: Protocol for the Microsoft Language Server Protocol (mslsp)

fields.name: rpc_response_error
documentation: function M.rpc_response_error(code: integer, message?: string, data?: any)
Creates an RPC response table `error` to be sent to the LSP response.
@*param* `message` — arbitrary message to send to server
@*param* `data` — arbitrary data to send to server

fields.name: set_log_level
documentation: Sets the global log level for LSP logging.
@*param* `level` — the case insensitive level name or number

fields.name: start
documentation: Create a new LSP client and start a language server or reuses an already
running client if one is found matching `name` and `root_dir`.
Attaches the current buffer to the client.
See |vim.lsp.ClientConfig| for all available options. The most important are:
- `name` arbitrary name for the LSP client. Should be unique per language server.
- `root_dir` path to the project root. By default this is used to decide if an existing client
should be re-used. The example above uses |vim.fs.root()| to detect the root by traversing
the file system upwards starting from the current directory until either a `pyproject.toml`
- `workspace_folders` list of `{ uri:string, name: string }` tables specifying the project root
folders used by the language server. If `nil` the property is derived from `root_dir` for
Language servers use this information to discover metadata like the
dependencies of your project and they tend to index the contents within the
To ensure a language server is only started for languages it can handle,
make sure to call |vim.lsp.start()| within a |FileType| autocmd.
Either use |:au|, |nvim_create_autocmd()| or put the call in a

fields.name: start_client
documentation: Starts and initializes a client with the given configuration.
fully initialized. Use `on_init` to do any actions once

fields.name: status
documentation: Consumes the latest progress messages from all clients and formats them as a string.
Empty if there are no clients or if no new messages

fields.name: stop_client
documentation: You can also use the `stop()` function on a |vim.lsp.Client| object.
By default asks the server to shutdown, unless stop was requested
already for this client, then force-shutdown is attempted.
@*param* `client_id` — id, list of id's, or list of |vim.lsp.Client| objects

fields.name: tagfunc
documentation: Provides an interface between the built-in client and 'tagfunc'.
When used with normal mode commands (e.g. |CTRL-]|) this will invoke
the "textDocument/definition" LSP method to find the tag under the cursor.
Otherwise, uses "workspace/symbol". If no results are returned from
any LSP servers, falls back to using built-in tags.
@*param* `pattern` — Pattern used to find a workspace symbol

fields.name: with
documentation: function lsp.with(handler: fun(err?: lsp.ResponseError, result: any, context: lsp.HandlerContext, config?: table):...unknown, override_config: table)
Function to manage overriding defaults for LSP handlers.
@*param* `override_config` — Table containing the keys to override behavior of the {handler}
